source_case: Barker v. Lull Engineering Co., 20 Cal. 3d 413 (1978)
doctrinal_framework: Barker five-factor risk-benefit test (California design-defect; adopted alongside consumer-expectations as the dual Barker standard)

source_verification.secondary_source_basis: Case digests inround 1 retrieval; Barker is a canonical teaching case in products-liability casebooks

case_background: Plaintiff Barker was injured while operating a high-lift loader manufactured by Lull Engineering, on steep terrain. He alleged the loader was defectively designed because it lacked outriggers, a roll bar, and a seat belt, making it unsafe for off-level operation. The California Supreme Court used the case to formulate the dual Barker design-defect standard: a product is defectively designed if (a) it fails to perform as safely as an ordinary consumer would expect OR (b) on risk-benefit analysis the benefits of the design do not outweigh the risks. The risk-benefit prong uses five factors enumerated below.

factors.text: The gravity of danger posed by the loader's challenged design — specifically the severity of injury from tipping, rollover, or operator ejection on uneven terrain.
factors.weight_justification: Court noted that loader rollover on steep ground produces severe operator injury — crush, impact, burial

factors.barker_factor_origin: Barker factor 2 (likelihood such danger would occur)
factors.text: The likelihood the danger would occur given foreseeable uses of the loader, including operation on the kind of terrain at issue.
factors.weight_justification: Court observed that loaders are foreseeably operated on uneven ground in construction work; probability is non-trivial

factors.barker_factor_origin: Barker factor 3 (mechanical feasibility of safer alternative design)
factors.text: The mechanical feasibility of a safer alternative design — specifically outriggers, roll bar, seat belt, or combination thereof.
factors.weight_justification: Court gave substantial weight to the technical achievability of the alternative designs; they were known in the industry

factors.barker_factor_origin: Barker factor 4 (financial cost of improved design)
factors.text: The financial cost of incorporating the proposed safer design elements into the loader manufacturing.
factors.weight_justification: Cost of the alternative design elements was moderate but not trivial; court weighed this against the magnitude of avoided harm

factors.barker_factor_origin: Barker factor 5 (adverse consequences of alternative design to product and consumer)
factors.text: The adverse consequences the proposed safer design would impose on the product and its consumer — weight, maneuverability, cost, or interference with legitimate off-level uses.
factors.weight_justification: Outriggers and roll bars impose some utility cost; court found this cost modest relative to safety gain

weight_sum_check.note: All five factor weights in [0.18, 0.22]; no single factor >0.5

question: Under Barker's risk-benefit prong, was the Lull high-lift loader defectively designed because it lacked outriggers, a roll bar, and a seat belt for off-level operation?

ground_truth.rationale: The California Supreme Court reversed the defense judgment and remanded; under the newly articulated Barker risk-benefit standard, the plaintiff could meet his burden by showing the five factors above, with burden shifting to the defendant to justify the challenged design. The court's risk-benefit framing weighed the severity and likelihood of harm against the feasibility and cost of the alternatives, and found the plaintiff's theory viable on the record.
ground_truth.note: Barker adopted a dual standard. Only the risk-benefit prong is captured in this FACET instance. The consumer-expectations prong is a separate test and is not measured here.

metadata.constructor: Venkateshwar Reddy Jambula (drafted with Claude Opus as programming and writing assistant; primary-text verification pending)
